questions.question: What is the definition of a data source in Notion?

options.text: A specific view configuration for a database
options.explanation: A data source is the set of pages that make up the data in the database, not the view configuration.

options.text: A shared link to a page outside of Notion
options.explanation: A data source refers to the internal collection of pages within a Notion database.

options.text: A set of pages in a database
options.explanation: A data source is defined as a set of pages in a database, such as multiple task pages.

options.text: A property type used to store text
options.explanation: A data source is the underlying data set, whereas properties are the attributes of the pages.

questions.question: How do you create a new database that links to an existing data source?

options.text: Select 'Copy data source' in the database menu
options.explanation: You must select 'Link to existing data source' to connect to a source without duplicating it.

options.text: Select 'Link to existing data source' and find the target database
options.explanation: This is the correct menu option to add an existing data source's pages to a new database.

options.text: Create a new page and use the /import command
options.explanation: The standard way is to select 'Link to existing data source' from the database creation menu.

options.text: Create a new empty database and manually type in the data
options.explanation: Linking allows you to pull in existing data without retyping it.

questions.question: How do access permissions work for linked data sources?

options.text: Each linked data source has its own independent access level
options.explanation: You cannot set a different level of access for each source; linked sources inherit the access level of the original database.

options.text: Linked data sources are always public to everyone
options.explanation: Linked data sources respect the access level of the original database, meaning they are restricted based on that permission.

options.text: Linked data sources respect the access level of the original database
options.explanation: If you connect a team's task database to your own, you retain the same access level you have in the original team database.

options.text: Linked data sources require Full access for the viewer
options.explanation: While Full access is required to manage data sources, viewing linked sources depends on the original database's permissions.

questions.question: Where are data sources that originate from the current database located in the 'Manage data sources' menu?

options.text: Under the 'Linked' section
options.explanation: Data sources you own are found under 'Sources', while external ones are under 'Linked'.

options.text: At the top of the menu
options.explanation: They are organized specifically under the 'Sources' tab within the menu.

options.text: Under the 'Sources' section
options.explanation: The 'Sources' section lists data sources you created or moved to the current database.

options.text: They are hidden unless you have Full access
options.explanation: They are visible under the 'Sources' section regardless of access level.

questions.question: What happens to existing views when you move a data source to another database?

options.text: All existing views are deleted automatically
options.explanation: You have the option to move the views or keep them in place; unmoved views become linked views.

options.text: All existing views are automatically converted to linked views
options.explanation: You can choose to move views; only the ones not moved along with the data source become linked views.

options.text: You can choose to move all views to the new destination or keep them where they are
options.explanation: The process allows you to preserve your current views, or migrate them to the new destination.

options.text: Existing views are merged with the new data source's views
options.explanation: Views are either moved, kept (becoming linked), or discarded, but not merged.

questions.question: What is the difference between moving a data source to a page versus moving it to another database?

options.text: Moving to a page deletes the data, while moving to a database keeps it
options.explanation: Moving to a page creates a new database within that page, while moving to a database makes it a data source for that database.

options.text: Moving to a page adds it as a database, while moving to a database adds it as a data source
options.explanation: This is the correct distinction: a page becomes the container (database) and a database becomes the data provider (data source).

options.text: Moving to a page makes it a linked data source, while moving to a database makes it a local source
options.explanation: Both actions move the source itself; the difference is the type of container it becomes (database vs data source).

options.text: There is no difference; they function identically
options.explanation: The resulting structure is different: a database container vs. a data source provider.

questions.question: When interacting with a linked data source, which of the following changes will NOT affect the original database?

options.text: Changing the title of the data source
options.explanation: Editing the title, properties, or pages will be reflected in the original database.

options.text: Deleting a view or changing a filter
options.explanation: Views, filters, sorts, and groups created in the linked database are local and do not affect the original.

options.text: Adding a new page to the data source
options.explanation: Adding pages to a linked data source will update the original database with the new content.

options.text: Modifying the properties of the data source
options.explanation: Modifying properties (like renaming a column) affects the original database.

questions.question: What access level is required to move a data source to a different location in the workspace?

options.text: Can edit access
options.explanation: While Can edit is needed to add data sources, Full access is specifically required to move them.

options.text: Full access
options.explanation: Only users with Full access in a database can move its data sources.

options.text: View only access
options.explanation: View only access is insufficient to perform management actions like moving data sources.

options.text: No access is required, anyone can move it
options.explanation: Strict permissions are in place; Full access is mandatory for moving data sources.

questions.question: Which menu option allows you to add a data source to an existing database?

options.text: Create new database
options.explanation: That option is for creating a new database, not adding to an existing one.

options.text: Manage data sources
options.explanation: You must click the slider icon and select 'Manage data sources' to add or link a source.

options.text: Export data
options.explanation: Export is for downloading data, not managing internal connections.

options.text: Duplicate database
options.explanation: Duplicate creates a copy, whereas Manage data sources allows linking or adding.

questions.question: How do you remove a linked data source from a database?

options.text: Select the data source and click Delete
options.explanation: Linked sources are found under the 'Linked' section, where you select 'X' to remove them.

options.text: Select 'X' next to the linked data source in the Manage data sources menu
options.explanation: In the 'Linked' section, clicking 'X' removes the linked data source from the current database.

options.text: Move it to Trash
options.explanation: Moving to Trash applies to data sources under the 'Sources' section (owned sources), not linked ones.

options.text: You cannot remove a linked data source, only owned ones
options.explanation: You can remove linked sources by selecting 'X', though doing so does not delete the original data source.

questions.question: If you share a database with a linked data source and the recipient cannot see it, what is the most likely cause?

options.text: The recipient has 'View only' access to the main database
options.explanation: Recipients need access to the *original* database containing the linked data source.

options.text: The recipient has 'Can edit' access to the main database
options.explanation: The issue is not their edit level, but their lack of access to the original source.

options.text: The recipient does not have access to the original database
options.explanation: Because linked sources respect the original database's access, the recipient must be granted access to that specific source.

options.text: The link has expired
options.explanation: The issue is permission-based, not expiration-based.
